title: Zeolitic inorganic-organic polymer electrolytes: a material based on poly(ethylene glycol) 600, SnCl4 and K4Fe(CN)6
abstract:
Zeolitic Inorganic±Organic Polymer
Electrolytes: A Material Based on
Poly(ethylene glycol) 600, SnCl4 and
K4Fe(CN)6²
Vito Di Noto1*,Davide Barreca1,Claudio Furlan2 and Lidia Armelao3
1Dipartimento di Chimica Inorganica, Metallorganica ed Analitica, UniversitaÁ di Padova, via Loredan 4, 35131-
Padova, Italy
2Centro Servizi Interdipartimenali CUGAS, UniversitaÁ di Padova, via Jappelli 1/A, 35131-Padova, Italy
3Centro di Studio sulla StabilitaÁ e ReattivitaÁ dei Composti di Coordinazione del CNR, via Marzolo 1, 35131-Padova,
Italy
ABSTRACT

This report describes the synthesis of a new zeolitic
inorganic±organic polymer electrolyte with the formula
[FexSny(CN)zClv(C2nH4n2On1)K1]. This material is
based
on
poly(ethylene
glycol)
600,
SnCl4
and
K4[Fe(CN)6], and is obtained via a sol®gel transition.
Mid and far Fourie than form infrared (FT-IR) studies,
analytical data and X-ray Photoelectron spectroscopy
(XPS) investigations allowed us to conclude that this
material is a mixed inorganic±organic network in which
Fe and Sn are bonded by CN bridges and tin atoms by
PEG 600 bridges. Mid-infrared (MIR) FT-IR investiga-
tions demonstrated that the polyether chains assume a
conformation of the TGT (T = trans, G = gauche) type.
Micrographs of the compound obtained by scanning
electron microscopy reveal that its morphology resembles
a smooth gummy paste. The conductivity of the material
at different temperatures was determined by impedance
spectroscopy (IS). Results indicated that the material
conducts ionically and that its conductivity is strongly
influenced by segmental motion of the polymer network.
Finally, this network shows a conductivity of ca.
3.7  10ÿ5S/cm at 25°C. Copyright Ó 2000 John Wiley
& Sons, Ltd.
KEYWORDS: inorganic±organic polymer electrolytes;
sol®gel transition; impedance spectroscopy; tin com-
plexes; iron cyanometalate derivatives
INTRODUCTION

In recent years, a large number of electrolyte
polymers have been prepared and studied in many
laboratories.[1] A book edited by Scrosati[2] pre-
sents applications of electrolyte polymers in the
production of batteries, sensors, etc. and points out
the tremendous potential technical application of
electrochemical energy storage systems based on
these materials.
With regard to battery chemistry, the replace-
ment of liquid electrolytes with nonaqueous thin
films of polymer electrolytes presents several
technological advantages.[2±5] (a) low manufactur-
POLYMERS FOR ADVANCED TECHNOLOGIES
Polym. Adv. Technol. 11, 108±121 (2000)
* Correspondence to: V. Di Noto, Dipartimento di Chimica
Inorganica, Metallorganica ed Analitica, UniversitaÁ di Padova, via
Loredan 4, 35131-Padova, Italy; e-mail: dinoto@ux1.unipd.it
² This paper was presented at the 2nd International Symposium on
Hi-tech Polymers and Polymeric Complexes (HPPC-II), Zhengzhou
University, China on 13±19 September 1999.
Copyright ã 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Received 12 September 1999
Accepted 10 December 1999
 ing cost and improved performance; (b) economy
of volume and mass; (c) structural and chemical
stability; (d) low toxicity; and (e) dual function as
an electrode separator. Moreover, the application of
polymer electrolytic systems in batteries leads to
much more stable and larger voltage outputs (up to
4.1±4.3V for lithium-conducting electrolytes) in
contrast with water-based electrolytes that deliver
output voltages that are limited by the electro-
chemical stability of water (in practice up to 2V).
The classic polymer electrolytes consist of
organic macromolecules (usually in the form of
polyetheral units) which are doped with inorganic
salts. [1, 2, 6, 7] In the past few years, in an attempt
to increase the conductivity of these materials, the
classical polymer electrolytes have been modified
by introducing inorganic oxides into the polymer
matrix. [8±10] It was suggested that the oxide
particles dissolved in the polymers create grain
boundaries which are responsible for the formation
of highly conductive layers at the polymer±ceramic
interfaces. Therefore, it was affirmed that these
(nano) composite polymer electrolytes (CPE) ex-
hibit an amorphous behaviour that is assured by
the inorganic component dissolved in the polymer
matrix. [11, 12] On the other hand, several studies
have been directed toward the insertion of inor-
ganic atoms in the backbone of polyethereal chains
of polymer electrolytes. [13, 14] These studies
recently yielded two innovative categories of
metallorganic hydrids with promising ionic con-
ductivities. The first class is formed by all those
electrolytic polymer products prepared by copoly-
merization of organic molecules with metal alkoxy
compounds. [13, 14]. In these materials the organic
macromolecules bridge together inorganic atoms
such as silicon and titanium, giving rise to three-
dimensional networks which conduct ionically;
their conductivity depends on the doping salt used
in the preparation of the system. The second class
includes all the materials named ªzeolitic inorgan-
ic±organic polymer electrolytesº (Z-IOPE). These
polymer electrolytes are structurally constituted by
inorganic negativelly charged clusters bonded
together by bridges of organic macromolecules
[15]. In general, the inorganic cluster site in the
network is formed by complexes of two or more
metal atoms. Z-IOPE systems are usually prepared
through sol®gel transitions and present high ionic
conductivities at room temperature (1.410ÿ3S/
cm). Representative examples of this latter class are
the Z-IOPE materials [15] prepared from K2PdCl4,
K3[Fe(CN)6] and poly(ethylene glycol) 600 (PEG
600) and the analogous product obtained using the
reagent combination K2PdCl4, K3[Co(CN)6] and
PEG 600.
This report describes the synthesis and char-
acterization of [FexSny(CN)zClv(C2nH4n2On1)K1],
a new Z-IOPE material prepared from PEG 600,
SnCl4 and K4[Fe(CN)6] using a reaction procedure
previously proposed in our laboratory [15]
The reaction characteristics are discussed, and
the following properties of the Z-IOPE are de-
scribed: (a) the structure by Fourier transform
infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectro-
scopy (XPS): (b) the thermal stability by thermo-
gravimetric
(TG)
measurements;
(c)
the
morphology
by
scanning
electron
microscopy
(SEM);
and
(d)
the
conductivity
at
different
temperatures by impedance spectroscopy (IS).
EXPERIMENTAL

Reagents
SnCl4 5H2O (Carlo Erba reagents) and K4Fe(CN)6
3H2O (Alfa reagents) were used as supplied. PEG
of molecular weight 600 (Aldrich ACS reagents)
was further purified by standard methods and
stored under argon on 4AÊ
activated molecular
sieves to prevent contamination by moisture. In
addition, all transfer and handling operations were
performed in an argon atmosphere.
Instrumentation and Methods
FT-IR spectra in mid-infrared (MIR) and far-
infrared (FIR) were recorded by using a Nicolet
5SxC spectrophotometer equipped with a trigly-
cine sulfate (TGS) detector at a nominal resolution
of 4cmÿ1 and collecting 600 scans; the Happ±
Genzel apodization method was adopted [16]. The
sample was distributed as a thin film between two
windows composed of KBr (for MIR) or polyethy-
lene (for FIR), and transferred into a sealed cell.
The amounts of Sn, Fe and K in the product
were determined by inductively coupled plasma
atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) by using
the method of standard additions and the emission
lines:
(Sn) = 189.926nm;
(Fe) = 259.940nm;
(K) = 766.490nm.
The
ICP-AES
measurements
were carried out by using a Spectroflame Modula
sequential
and
simultaneous
spectrometer
equipped with a capillary cross-flow nebulizer
(Spectro Analytical, Kleve, Germany). The element
determinations were performed using a plasma
power of 1.2kW, a radiofrequency generator of
27.12MHz and an argon gas flow with nebulizer,
auxiliary and coolant set at 1, 0.5 and 14l/min,
respectively. The solution suitable for ICP-AES
analysis
was
obtained
by
microwave
sample
digestion using a CEM MDS-2100 microwave
sample preparation system and by treatment of
132.4mg of the resulting product with a solution of
54% (v/v) HNO3 (70%), 31% (v/v) HCl (37%), 15%
(v/v) H2O.
TG analysis was performed using a Perkin
Elmer TGS-2 thermobalance, operating in a nitro-
gen atmosphere inside a Braun MB-150 I/II dry
box. The TG profile was recorded under a working
N2 dynamic flux of 50cm3/min and at a heating
rate of 20°C/min.
The morphology of the samples was examined
by SEM using a Cambridge Stereoscan 250 Mark 1
scanning electron microscope at an acceleration
voltage of 20kV. The samples were coated with
graphite film using a Balzer mod. Med. 010(6) unit
operating at 0.2mbar from 20 to 80°C.
Copyright ã 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Polym. Adv. Technol., 11, 108±121 (2000)
Zeolitic Inorganic±Organic Polymer Electrolytes / 109

Zeolitic Inorganic±Organic Polymer Electrolytes / 109
was carried out by SEM in conjunction with an
energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDX) EDAX
PW9800 operating with a Si/Li detector.
AC impedance measurements were carried out
in the 20Hz to 1MHz frequency range by using a
computer-controlled HP4284A precision LCR me-
ter. The sample was prepared as a pellet and placed
in a home-made cell consisting of two solid
cylindrical gold-plated stainless steel electrodes
joined by a teflon ring containing a compartment
for the sample. The diameter of the electrode±
electrolyte contact surface and the distance be-
tween the electrodes were determined by micro-
meter measurements and no correction was made
for thermal expansion of the cell. The temperature
was measured with an accuracy greater than
A Perkin Elmer F 5600ci spectrometer with
monochromatized AlKa radiation (1486.6eV) was
used for the XPS analyses. The working pressure
was less than 1.810ÿ9mbar. The spectrometer was
calibrated by assuming the binding energy (BE) of
the Au 4f7/2 line at 83.9eV with respect to the Fermi
level. After a Shirley-type background subtraction
[17], the raw spectra were fitted using a nonlinear
least-square fitting program adopting Gaussian±
Lorentzian shapes for all the peaks. The atomic
compositions were evaluated using sensitivity
factors as provided by F V5.4A software.
After recording a wide scan spectrum for each
sample in the 0±1300eV range, detailed scans were
run for the C1s and O1s regions. The charge
compensation was introduced by assuming a BE
of 102.4eV for the Si2p photopeak of the silicon
resin present as a surface contaminant [18].
Synthesis of [FexSny(CN)zClvC2nH4n2On1)Kl]
The electrolytic complex was synthesized by mix-
ing solutions A and B as follows. Solution A,
colorless, was obtained by dissolving 0.4951g
(1.41  10ÿ3mol) of SnCl4 5H2O in 4.00g of water,
followed by the addition of 2.01g of PEG 600.
Solution B, with a transparent yellow color, was
prepared by dissolving 0.3043g (7.2  10ÿ4mol) of
K4Fe(CN)6 3H2O in 4.03g of water, followed by
Upon mixing solutions A and B, a yellow gel
and a transparent liquid immediately formed. This
mixture was filtered and washed with water under
an argon atmosphere, then dried at 50°C and
10ÿ5mbar for a week and stored under an argon
atmosphere. The final product had the appearance

mixture presented the weight composition re-

(step a of Scheme 1) instantaneously gave rise to a
heterogeneous mixture containing a yellow gel and
a transparent solution. This transition probably
takes place due to the following consecutive
substitution reactions at the Sn metal center:

based on poly (ethylene glycol) 600, SnCl4 and K4Fe(CN)6.

Indeed, it is well known [19, 20] that the Clÿ
ions in SnCl4 can readily be substituted by either
the nitrogen atom of cyanometallate [Fe(CN)6]4ÿ
complex or the hydroxyl group of water or PEG
600. After complete elimination of water from the
material under vacuum (step c of Scheme 1), only
the hydroxyl groups of PEG 600 remain available
for coordination of the tin metal atom. Therefore,
we believe that water probably acts as a catalyst in
this reaction, promoting the elimination of both
Table 2 reports the analytical data obtained for
the final product together with the molar ratios
nSn/nPEG, nFe/nPEG, nK/nPEG and nCN/nPEG in the
reaction mixture and in the Z-IOPE. It is to be

observed that nFe/nPEG and nCN/nPEG are practi-
cally the same in the reaction mixture and in the
final product, indicating that no loss of cyanome-
tallate in the clear solution took place. On the
contrary, nSn/nPEG and nK/nPEG ratios are smaller
in the synthesized material than in the reaction
mixture, indicating that K and low molecular
weight derivatives of Sn were lost in the transpar-
ent solution, which consisted mainly of water.
Thermal Analysis and Morphology
The thermogram of the Z-IOPE material depicted
in Fig. 1 clearly shows only two mass eliminations.
The first is observed in the 190±500°C temperature
range and is attributed to the decomposition of the
PEG 600 moieties present in the material [15]. The
second, which is detected in the 690±920°C range,
could reflect the elimination of both KCl by
sublimation [21] and of cyanogen (CN)2, the
decomposition product of cyanide from the cyano-
metallate complex [22, 23]. The TG measurements
indicate that the Z-IOPE material is thermally
The morphology of the inorganic±organic poly-
mer electrolyte was studied by SEM. The micro-
graphs reported in Fig. 2 show the morphology of
the Z-IOPE at two magnifications. The surface of
this material has the appearance of a smooth
gummy paste (Fig. 2a); empty microcavities and
perceptible roughness in bulk are completely
absent (Fig. 2b). Several portions of the sample
were analyzed by X-ray fluorescence analysis with
energy dispersive spectroscopy (XRD-EDS), results
of which showed that C, O, Sn, Fe, N, K and Cl are
uniformly distributed in the bulk material. Further-
more, backscattered images indicated that no
microcrystals are incorporated in the bulk material,
material is a highly homogeneous system.
XPS investigations were carried out in an attempt
to determine the oxidation state of the metal atoms
present in the material. However, owing to their
low concentration in the Z-IOPE material, no metal
atom peaks were detected by this method (see
Table 2). Moreover, the O1s peak due to poly(-
ethylene glycol) shows a single component cen-
